serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Cơm Thịt Kho Trứng weighs approximately 550 g and typically includes a serving of steamed white rice topped with two to three pieces of braised pork belly and one or two hard-boiled eggs in caramelised braising sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Pork Belly With Egg Rice (Cơm Thịt Kho Trứng) — HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95 for an exact dish match. Saturated fat (3.27 g/100 g), trans fat (0.09 g/100 g), and vitamin C (0.36 mg/100 g) were tagged to GrabFood (confidence 0.70) in the input record; GrabFood is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. These three values are therefore treated as unverified estimates and flagged in notes. The HPB Foodo record does not independently report saturated fat, trans fat, or vitamin C for this dish; these values are retained as the best available approximation pending a verified approved-source update, and are assigned confidence 0.50 to reflect their unverified status. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white jasmine rice analogue used as dominant carbohydrate component of Cơm Thịt Kho Trứng
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cơm Thịt Kho Trứng as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white jasmine rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 per the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283). The pork belly and egg components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the overall GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 138.5) / 100 = 99.7, rounded to 99.6. Carbohydrate per serving = 25.19 g/100g × 550 g = 138.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 1,270 mg sodium, approaching 55% of the 2,300 mg daily limit, with additional sodium from fish sauce in the braising liquid likely underrepresented in the composite figure.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request reduced fish sauce in preparation, and note that the potassium content (327 mg/100 g) provides a partial offsetting benefit consistent with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice dominates the carbohydrate load at 25.19 g/100 g with a GI of approximately 72; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 138.5 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of approximately 99.6, which is clinically significant for postprandial glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce portion size substantially (150–200 g), substitute brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower the GI, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glycaemic response; the dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g and protein at 6.5 g/100 g provide modest glycaemic buffering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.27 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.09 g/100 g; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 18 g of saturated fat, exceeding the AHA recommended daily limit of 13 g for a 2,000 kcal diet, with pork belly's high saturated fat content directly linked to LDL-C elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to a small portion (100–150 g of the pork belly component only), trim visible fat before consumption, and consider substituting pork belly with a leaner protein such as chicken breast or tofu to substantially reduce saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acids) and saturated fat, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-alpha, IL-6) that can exacerbate RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit pork belly and substitute with anti-inflammatory proteins such as oily fish (salmon, mackerel) or chicken breast to reduce omega-6-driven inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cơm Thịt Kho Trứng (Braised Pork Belly with Egg Rice) is a classic Vietnamese comfort dish consisting of steamed white rice served alongside slow-braised pork belly and hard-boiled eggs cooked in a caramelised sauce made from fish sauce, coconut water, sugar, garlic, shallots, and black pepper. The braising process renders the pork belly tender and deeply flavoured, with a glossy amber sauce that coats both the meat and eggs. The dish delivers a savoury-sweet flavour profile with a rich, unctuous texture from the pork belly fat and a firm, flavour-saturated egg. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat (including 3.27 g saturated fat), and 25.19 g carbohydrate dominated by white rice. Dietary fibre is 3.7 g/100 g and sodium is 231 mg/100 g, though actual sodium may be higher due to fish sauce. A standard 550 g plate delivers approximately 902 kcal and 138.5 g of carbohydrate, making portion control critical for individuals managing blood glucose or cardiovascular risk. The dish is culturally significant in Vietnamese households, particularly during Tết (Lunar New Year), and is widely available in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore. It is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content, and patients with rheumatoid arthritis, cardiovascular disease, or Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with full-portion consumption.

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact dish match. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values are retained from the input record but flagged as unverified estimates (original source GrabFood is forbidden under Veritas source policy); these are assigned reduced confidence 0.50 pending a verified approved-source update. GI estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (white jasmine rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, which is the standard analogous-estimate approach for composite rice dishes.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Vietnamese dish is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish not reported in HPB Foodo. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and are not available from a directly applicable approved source for this composite preparation. 4. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB Foodo (confirmed zero per approved source); this is noted as potentially anomalous given the presence of egg yolk and pork belly, and may reflect a data limitation in the composite dish record rather than a true zero — downstream users should treat this value with caution. Confirmed zero per HPB Foodo. 5. saturated_fat_g (3.27 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.09 g/100 g), and vitamin_c_mg (0.36 mg/100 g) were originally tagged to GrabFood (confidence 0.70) in the input record. GrabFood is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. These values are retained as the best available approximation but are flagged as unverified estimates at confidence 0.50 pending a verified approved-source update. The HPB Foodo record does not independently report these three fields for this dish. 6. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); no direct GI measurement for Cơm Thịt Kho Trứng as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026.

Root issue: saturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and vitamin_c_mg in per_100g were originally sourced from GrabFood (a forbidden source) in the input record. These values are now explicitly identified as unverified estimates from a forbidden source in nutrient_source.source_match_notes and in notes, with confidence reduced to 0.50. The HPB Foodo approved source is declared as the primary evidence basis for all core macros. The nutrient_source title and organization now unambiguously reference only the approved HPB Foodo source.
